If you or someone you love was just hit by a semi truck in Atlanta, we want you to know—you are not alone, and what happens in the next few days matters more than you might realize.

Atlanta's massive highway system means truck accidents happen every single day on I-75, I-85, I-20, and I-285. What makes these cases different from regular car accidents is the evidence involved: black box data, driver logs, cargo records, and federal compliance documents that trucking companies can legally destroy if no one acts quickly to preserve them.

You may be dealing with pain, shock, insurance calls, and a mountain of stress right now. The most important thing you can do is connect with an attorney who understands trucking law before that evidence disappears.

WreckMatch can help you get matched with an experienced truck accident attorney in Georgia—at no cost to you to get started.

reddit_title: Got hit by a semi truck in Atlanta — what evidence should I be trying to preserve right now?
reddit_body:
Semi truck accidents in Georgia are legally very different from regular car accidents. Trucking companies are regulated under federal FMCSA rules, which means there are driver logs, electronic logging device (ELD) records, black box data, and inspection reports that could be critical to your case. The problem is that some of this data has a short retention window and can be legally overwritten or destroyed if you don't act fast—sometimes within 30 days.

The first thing you should do is send a spoliation letter through an attorney to the trucking company demanding they preserve all records related to the incident. You'll also want to document the scene if you haven't already, get witness contact info, and avoid giving recorded statements to any insurance adjuster—theirs or yours—before speaking with a lawyer.

If you're in the Atlanta area and need help finding a truck accident attorney quickly, a free matching service like wreckmatch.com can connect you with attorneys who specifically handle these cases in Georgia. Time really does matter here, so don't wait to get legal advice even if you're not sure you have a case yet.
